contextual.title: A Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) - Based Method to Improve Antibiotic Prescribing for Pneumonia
contextual.text:
Summary: Pneumonia, or lung infection, is usually treated with antibiotics targeted against the organisms that the physician guesses are causing the problem. The determination of the exact cause of a patient's pneumonia is difficult. The problem is that the two major causes of community-acquired pneumonia are not easily distinguished on clinical grounds and are best treated by different antibiotics. The investigators hypothesize that antibiotic therapy can be targeted and improved by doing polymerase chain reaction (PCR) testing of nose swabs to identify probable implicated organisms and their antibiotic resistance patterns. This pilot study will be important to ensure that the laboratory testing is functional and that the emergency department-laboratory communication is optimal prior to doing a full-fledged randomized clinical trial.
Inclusion criteria: inclusion criteria: 

 presumed community-acquired pneumonia as diagnosed by the attending emergency department physician 

 
Exclusion criteria: : 

 age > 6 months 

 immunodeficiency (primary, advanced HIV) 

 cystic fibrosis 

 malignancy 

 known cardiac or lung defects 

 bronchiectasis 

 previous pneumonia or lung abscess in past 6 months 

 conditions requiring treatment with immune suppressants

Pneumonia.definition: Infection of the lung often accompanied by inflammation.
Infection of the lung often accompanied by inflammation.
